Disjoint set collections
DS 2018/2019

• Plane passengers
– Priorities:
• Buisness-class
• Persons travelling with children / with
reduced mobility
• Other passengers
• Planes approaching the airport
– Priorities:
• Emergences
• Fuel level
• Distance to the airport
abstract

• Objects: data structures where the
elements are called atoms; any
atom has key-field called priority.
• Elements are stores function of their
priorities and not their position.

• read
– input: priority queue C
– output: the atom from C with the highest
priority
• delete
– input: priority queue C
– output: C from which the atom with the
highest priority has been deleted
• insert
– input: priority queue C and an atom at
– output: C where the atom at has been added

x
a[i]

• Theorem: A maxHeap with n keys has the height
O(log n).
• Proof:
– The complete binary tree properties are used.
– Let h a maxHeap height with n keys.
– There are
keys of depth i, for i = 0, ..., h-1 and at least
one key of depth h:
– It follows:

• The heap root is deleted (it correspons
to the atom with the highest probability).
• The algorithm has three stages:
– The root key is replaced with the key of
the last node;
– The last node is deleted;
– The maxHeap property is repaired.

• The new key is inserted in a new
node.
• Algorithm has three stages:
– A new node is added as the rightmost
node on the last level;
– The new key is inserted in this node;
– The maxHeap property is repaired.

procedure insert(a, n, key)
begin
n  n+1
a[n-1]  key
j  n-1
heap  false
while ((j > 0) and not heap) do
k  [(j-1)/2]
if (a[j] > a[k])
then swap(a[j], a[k])
j  k
else heap  true
end

procedure delete(a, n)
begin
a[0]  a[n-1]
n  n-1
j  0
heap  false
while ((2*j+1 < n) and not heap) do
k  2*j+1
if ((k < n-1) and (a[k] < a[k+1]))
then k  k+1
if (a[j] < a[k])
then swap(a[j], a[k])
j  k
else heap  true
end

• The insert/delete operations require the
time O(h) = O(log n)

Applications:

abstract data type

• objects: disjoint sets collections (partitions)
of a universe set
• operations:
– find()
• input: a collection C, an element i from the universe set
• output: the subset of C to which i belongs
– union()
• input: a collection C, two elements i and j from universe
• output: C where the components of a i and j are joint
– singleton()
• input: a collection C, an element i from universe
• output: C where the component of i has i as unique
element.
“union-find”

• The “union-find” structure
– universe set = {0,1, ..., n-1}
– subset = tree
– collection = forest
– Forest reprezentation by parent link
“union-find”

“union-find”
procedure singleton(C, i)
begin
C.parent[i]  -1
end

“union-find”
function find(C, i)
begin
temp  i
while (C.parent[temp] >= 0) do
temp  C.parent[temp]
return temp
end

“union-find”
procedure union(C, i, j)
begin
ri  find(i)
rj  find(j)
if (ri != rj) then C.parent[rj]

 ri
end

• Solution for the degenerated trees
problem.
• Mechanism:
– Store the number of node of the tree
(with negative sign).
– Tree flatting.

• example:

parent

procedure union(C, i, j)
begin
ri  find(i); rj  find(j)
while (C.parent[i] >= 0) do
temp  i; i  C.parent[i]; C.parent[temp] ri
while (C.parent[j] >= 0) do
temp  j; j  C.parent[j]; C.parent[temp] rj
if (C.parent[ri] > C.parent[rj])
then C.parent[rj]  C.parent[ri]+C.parent[rj]
C.parent[ri]  rj
else C.parent[ri]  C.parent[ri]+C.parent[rj]
C.parent[rj]  ri
end

• Theorem: Starting from an empty
collection, any sequence of M
operations “union” and “find” over N
elements has complexity O(N+M lg*N).
– lg*N = number of logarithms until 1 is
obtained.
